feed.title: Vashudev Dhama | Software Developer
feed.subtitle: Vashudev Dhama is a software developer, specializing in building (and occasionally designing) static websites, cross-platform mobile applications and everything in between.

entry.title: Learn how to generate static sites with Jekyll Part 2

link.title: Learn how to generate static sites with Jekyll Part 2

entry.content:
Hi there! Welcome Back. In this tutorial, we’ll cover the basics of Jekyll Layouts and Includes.

In “Learn how to generate static sites with Jekyll Part 1”, we’ve covered Jekyll Setup, Liquid template and Front matter. If you have not read, I would strongly suggest you to read the Part-1 post before proceeding further.

1. Layouts

Layout is a better choice for multi-page websites. We create layouts in a directory names _layouts under root directory. Let’s create our first layout _layouts/default.html and write in the following content:


<!doctype html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title>{{ page.title }}</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    {{ content }}
  </body>
</html>

You will notice that this file looks similar to our previous index.html file created in Part-1, except that there’s no front matter and there’s only content variable inside the body tag. Note: content is a special variable used to access the value of rendered content of the page it’s called on.

Now, to make use of this layout in our index.html, we can set a layout variable as front matter and our index.html file will look like this:


---
layout: default
title: Home
greet: Jekyll
---

<h1>Hello {{ page.greet }}</h1>

Note that we can access the page front matter from the layout (title variable is set in the index.html but is output in layout. Now save all changes, and start the jekyll serve. On localhost:4000, you’ll see that the site renders everything like before. Let’s create another page using the same _layouts/default.html layout. We’ll create about.md page to show our introduction. The root/about.md file looks like this:

---
layout: default
title: About
---
# About page

This page tells you a little bit about me.

Run jekyll serve (if not already running), head over to localhost:4000/about.html. Cool! There is our about page just using about.md file(as we have already set our default layout).

2. Includes

The include tag is used to include content from another file stored in root/_include folder. It is useful for having a single source code that repeats around the site. Syntax for includes is like: {% include filename.html %}

Let’s take the example of navigation. Since navigation should be on every page, let’s create it using the concept of includes.

Create a file for navigation at _includes/navigation.html having the below content:

<nav>
  <a href="/">Home</a>
  <a href="/about.html">About</a>
</nav>

Now, we need to modify _layouts/default.html to add the navigation:


<!doctype html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title>{{ page.title }}</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    {% include navigation.html %}
    {{ content }}
  </body>
</html>

Open the localhost:4000 in your browser, you’ll see a basic navigation. Try to switch between pages.

That’s all for this post. We’ve learnt pretty important stuff about basics of Layouts and Includes. Keep an eye for Part-3 till then go on and try various layout and includes stuff to make the concept clear.
entry.summary: Hi there! Welcome Back. In this tutorial, we’ll cover the basics of Jekyll Layouts and Includes.

entry.title: Learn how to generate static sites with Jekyll Part 1

link.title: Learn how to generate static sites with Jekyll Part 1

entry.content:
In this tutorial series, you’ll learn how to build your first Jekyll site from scratch. Note: It would be nice to have fundamental HTML and CSS knowledge to gain the most from the tutorials.

Jekyll is a static site generator suitable for Blog and Portfolio static websites. It creates static websites from markup files using layouts.

Let’s get into the Jekyll world!

Setup

1. Installation

Jekyll need Ruby on your machine to run. Head over the post and follow the instructions. Note: To explore more on installation, visit https://jekyllrb.com/docs/installation/

2. Setup

  • Open your terminal and change the directory to a suitable path where you want to initialize and store your Jekyll project.
  • Run the follwing command to initialize the Jekyll project folder.
jekyll new root

Note: Here “root” is the parent directory name for my Jekyll project. You can use any name you want, but for this post we’ll use root only.

3. Create home page

It’s time to say Hello Jekyll!

Let’s add our first file index.html in the root directory with the following content:

  <!doctype html>
  <html>
    <head>
      <meta charset="utf-8">
      <title>Home</title>
    </head>
    <body>
     	<h1>Hello Jekyll!</h1>
    </body>
  </html>

3. Build the site

We need to build the site before we can view it. Now, we have two options to build the site: one is jekyll build and the other is jekyll serve. During website development, use jekyll serve which will run your website on local server at localhost:4000. For production build, we use jekyll build. So, for now, run jekyll serve in the root directory of your site to build it and serve it on local serve. Now move to localhost:4000 in your browser. Tadaa!! Hello Jekyll.

Liquid Template Language

Role of Jekyll starts to get intereting from here. Actually, Liquid is a template language which has three main parts:

1. Objects:

Objects, denoted by double curly braces {{ and }}, shows Liquid where to output content. Example: {{ page.title }} This would output page.title variable on the page.

2. Tags:

Tags, denoted by curly braces and percent signs {% and %}, are used for logic and control flow for templates. Example:

{% if page.showContent %}
  <div class = “content”>
    Content
  </div>
{% endif %}

This will show the content only if page.showContent is true.

3. Filters:

Modify the output of Liquid object with filters. Example:

{{ “first initial made capitalize” | capitalize }} By, adding Filter “capitalize”, this would output as “First initial made capitalize”. Note: Observe that filter is separated by a vertical bar | within an output. Now, to use Liquid in our root/index.html file, let’s add the following code in body of our index.html file.

<h1>{{ “Hello Jekyll!” | smartify }}</h1>

Note: If you refresh your web page in the browser, you will see the content the same as in the code. So for Liquid template to work, we need to add front matter so that Jekyll would be able to process Liquid.

Front Matter

Front matter is a snippet of YAML(YAML is a human friendly data serialization standard for all programming languages), used to set variables for the page and written between two triple-dashed lines at the top of a file like this:

---
name: Jekyll
---

We can access front matter variables under the page variable like this:

{{ page.name }}

Now let’s include front matter and change the <title> and <body> in index.html using front matter like this:

---
title: Home
greet: Jekyll
---

<!doctype html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title>Learn how to generate static sites with Jekyll Part 1</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <h1> Hello </h1>
  </body>
</html>

Run jekyll serve (if not currently serving) and head over the browser’s local host page localhost:4000 There you go! We have setup the Jekyll engine, built our site with working Liquid templating using the front matter.

That’s all for the post. Stay tuned for Part-2 and keep exploring.
entry.summary: In this tutorial series, you’ll learn how to build your first Jekyll site from scratch. Note: It would be nice to have fundamental HTML and CSS knowledge to gain the most from the tutorials.

entry.title: Jekyll Setup on Debian or Ubuntu

link.title: Jekyll Setup on Debian or Ubuntu

entry.content:
This post will take you through the complete step-by-step procedure of installing and making Jekyll static site builder functional on your machine.

1. Installation process:

Ruby Development Environment:

Jekyll require Ruby version 2.5.0 or above, including all development headers. Check ruby version:

  • Open your terminal ( CTRL + ALT + T).

  • Check for Ruby environment using the command ruby -v in your terminal. If the command doesn’t output the required version of Ruby 2.5.0 or above, then do the following:

    • Run the following command in your terminal: 
      
sudo apt-get install ruby-full

    • Now run the following commands in your terminal:

      
ruby -v
gem -v

      They both should return appropriate versions available on the system.

Make sure that “GCC” and “Make” are installed on your system ( check by running gcc -v, g++ -v and make -v in your terminal). If not, head over to their official website for installation instructions: GCC and Make

Install all the required dependencies before we install Jekyll by running the following command in your terminal:


sudo apt-get install ruby-full build-essential zlib1g-dev

It’s a good practice to create a gem installation directory for your user account. Run the following commands in your terminal. These will add environment variables to your ~/.bashrc.


echo '# Install Ruby Gems to ~/gems' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export GEM_HOME="$HOME/gems"' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/gems/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

Finally install Jekyll:


gem install jekyll bundler.

That’s all, you are good to go to start using Jekyll.

2. Check Jekyll for a test site.

  • Open your terminal and change the directory to a suitable path where you want to initialize and store your Jekyll project. (We’ll use “testSite” as our root directory for project).

  • Run the following command to initialize a new Jekyll site:


jekyll new testSite
  • Change into the new directory:

cd testSite
  • Now run the following command to build and host the site on local server:

bundle exec jekyll serve

  • Browse to localhost:4000 in your browser. There you go, you should see the default Welcome to Jekyll page. Note: You can stop the server by pressing CTRL + C in your terminal window which is showing server running. So now you have:

  • Installed the Jekyll and related development environment on your system.
  • Built and hosted the default page on your local server. That’s all for this post, keep an eye for more such posts.
entry.summary: This post will take you through the complete step-by-step procedure of installing and making Jekyll static site builder functional on your machine.
